speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 15th, 1996. We are in Montreal, Canada, and I will be interviewing Eva Roden, nee Vonchovska. Roden is spelled R-O-D-E-N. Vonchovska is spelled V-O-N-C-H-O-V-Z-K--

speaker: interviewee
text: S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: S-K.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Eva. My name is Paula Weitzman. That's spelled W-E-I-T-Z-M-A-N. This interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Roden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Roden?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-D-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Vonchovska. V-O-N-C-H-O-V-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prague in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 1st, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back to Prague, in the early and mid '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about the Prague that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Prague was beautiful, and it was my home town. And I just love-- loved living there, even as a little girl. I-- I loved everything about Prague. I couldn't imagine living anywhere else. I mean, the thought never crossed my mind that I would live anywhere else. And, funnily enough, what everybody today refers to the beauty of Prague-- when you grow up, and it's-- they say that's beautiful, you never really see it that way. You think it's all right, that's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What neighborhood did you grow up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in a normal, middle class neighborhood, on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very close to a park. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your last address in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: My last address was Prague 1, [CZECH], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Kozi. K-O-Z-I. Number Five. And that was basically what turned into the Prague ghetto later, when the Germans came. Which it wasn't, then. Then it was central Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you lived on Kozi number one?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we lived in Kozi, I was about 10-- 10, 11. Until we were transported-- until 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you walk me into your home on Kozi number 5?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It-- it had-- it was on the third floor. It had a large foyer. And to the right, you walked into the kitchen. Behind the kitchen were the maid's room. And to the left was a door that led to the living room. From the living room, there was a big double door that led to sort of a-- to a dining room. And then behind the dining room, again, a connecting door that led to my room. And from my room, sort of at a right angle, you walked into my parents' bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: The parents' bedroom had a bathroom. And in the foyer, there was a toilet. What else can I tell you? There was a gray bench with red webbing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a mirror with a small shelf and an umbrella stand. Do you remember anything regarding the home that you had lived in earlier as a younger child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was also-- it was a very sunny apartment, smaller. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had my own room there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a living room-- living room, dining room, and my parent's bedroom, and a bathroom, and a kitchen, and a maid's room. And my room was off the hall, small, with a tall stove. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had a crib there that I remember. And I had a canary, which somebody hung out of the window one day to give him a chance to have some sun. And the door flew open, and the canary was gone. And that was a big tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your canary's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. It had no name. It was-- I don't know-- maybe he had a name. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this home located in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- now I'll have to spell it-- that was Prague 12. And it was S-- with an accent-- M-I-L-O-V-S-K-E-H-O four, number 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we go back to your home on Kozi Street, which was your last-- last home where you lived in Prague, if you walk me down the street on-- where your home was, what do we see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was located in a-- what is now a very touristy part of Prague. It was two blocks from the old squaring, from-- and it was a very short street which led from-- there were about three houses on each side. And across from us was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was an office which stamped silver or gold. A state office for stamping, giving the official stamps to anything manufactured out of silver or gold. And then it faced on a little square, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a round square. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I could tell you exactly how my home was furnished. It was-- it was done in-- in oak furniture, which came from [INAUDIBLE]. My mother was very proud of it, and it was made to measure. And it was sort of brownish, like cherry wood. And it had no bed. It was a couch, which was a very modern idea. And the couch was-- it had three mattresses, and they were covered in red. And they had a huge desk, which was-- the top of the desk was black linoleum, which was not a very practical thing, but still. And there was a library behind glass doors, and a glass table, like here. But the glass was, again, black. My mother got hold of some sort of interior decorator, and it was not really suitable for a young kid, but that's what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I had two chairs in red leather, very similar to-- to the one that's here. And it was like pleated-- the-- the backs were pleated red leather, tomato red. You know what I mean by pleated? Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's about all. It was a nuisance, beca-- and there was a big wardrobe there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if you wanted to make the bed, you had to turn around the mattresses, and the sheets were in a drawer underneath. But I never did it, so-- [LAUGHS] until the war, so then it didn't matter. So that was my room. The dining room was very formal, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and it had a round table with four chairs, and-- and two chairs in a corner. And it had a glass-enclosed sort of curio cabinet, where my mother collected all kinds of china, Rosenthal, and [INAUDIBLE], and the figurines. And they were there. And there was a big bay window. In the big bay window was a small table with two armchairs. And a huge buffet. And on the buffet was a clock which had a-- sounded every quarter of an hour, half an hour, and an hour. And it sounded like-- it had a sound like Big Ben in London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were two silver candelabra on each end, and a chandelier. And the living room had huge armchairs, sort of-- they called it-- they didn't call it the living room. They called it the gentlemen's room, for some reason. That was not in our family. Everywhere in Prague, it was the gentlemen's room. And it had two round tables, and very deep armchairs. And-- and again, a library behind glass. Everything and locked in this case. And rugs. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of books were found in the libraries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had access only to my library. The rest was really locked. I don't know what they were. They were some novels my mother read, and there were some historical novels, and some encyclopedias. But my books were my books. That was my treasure, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I read voraciously from-- from the moment I could-- I could read. When I was seven, I guess-- six, seven. And I read everything I could hand-- lay my hands on. I read the Czech classics, the fairy tales, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mostly, when you were growing up, you read either the Czech classic books, and the-- the poetry, and fairy tales, and novels about, you know, young girls, things like that. But I think I read a little bit beyond my age group, at that point, whatever I could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your neighbors on Kozi 5?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no contact with your neighbors at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope. No. No. The only people we knew-- and I mean, knew-- we-- we knew the-- we knew to say hello to was the landlord. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was it. I didn't know my neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Growing up in Prague, you attended school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the education in your schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the school-- you know, the-- the curriculum was unified from west to east, all through Czechoslovakia, I mean, in elementary school. So you learn how to read and write, and you had something that was called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] civic, you would call it here, you know, about obeying the law, obeying traffic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But basically, the emphasis was on reading, writing, and spelling. Arithmetic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in grade two, we had draw-- I lose my voice, sorry-- we had drawing. And we had something called-- that was called-- I don't know how we would call it. We learned how to sew, put on a button, or darn a sock. It was hopeless.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did we do? And with the drawing, it was hopeless with me, too. And then we had singing, music. This was a public school in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: A public school. Public school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only the public schools had the same curriculum and you-- there were no private schools, as far as I-- no. There were maybe some convent schools, but I don't know of any private school. They had to follow orders of the Ministry of Schooling-- of Education, the curriculum. We had four reports and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every year. And we were-- in grade school, we were marked by numbers, one, two, three, four, five. And when you had a five, you had to repeat. There was no-- no question about it. Nobody argued, nobody asked not to repeat. You were-- you repeat. We had large classes, and it-- and it was segregated as far as boys and girls are concerned. You didn't have accreditation. You had only accreditation of schools later. And the gymnasium, if you wanted. So these grade schools were not of your choice. You went to the school of your neighborhood [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other Jewish students in your classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my class, in my grade school, there was nobody. Then in-- then when I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nine, I-- then you had the choice, you know. I had to-- I was in a sort of accelerated program, and you had to make an exam if you wanted to go into a gymnasium. The gymnasium, you didn't have to go in your neighborhood, but you had a choice. Your parents had a choice, that is. You can go to either a gymnasium, for which you needed the exam. Or you could go to a sort of junior high, you would call it here, for four years. And with it-- and that-- this way, the schooling would be finished. Normally, you had five years of grade school. I had only four, because I was in sort of an accelerated class. And then you-- with the gymnasium, you had a choice. You could have a classical gymnasium, where they had Latin and Greek. You could have Re-- what they call Realgymnasium, where you had only Latin. Then you had a Reformgymnasium, where you had neither Latin nor Greek, but you had French. And now-- those were the choices. And you could choose any school-- I mean your parents-- in Prague. Whichever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents-- my mother chose an all girls school, because that's where my three cousins went. So-- and I-- I had to write an entrance exam at the end of grade four, which I passed. You write it in the morning. It's a four, five-hour exam. How old were you then, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about-- I was exactly nine. And you write an exam in Czech, and sort of a dictee. And you write a short story on the topic they give you, and-- and math. It was a four-hour exam, and they told you that same afternoon whether you passed or not. And if you would speak Czech, I would still tell you what I did-- the sentence I had in my Czech spelling, which was very, sort of-- you know, in Czech language, the-- the difficulty is with i and igrec-- i and y. And there are too many exceptions. So they gave you three or four sentences which were really difficult for small children. I went to an all girls school, and the last year, I had a year of Latin, which was taught very well, because I still-- I-- I never forgot what I learned. And we had an-- we never went to school in the afternoon. We went to school from 8:00 to-- in grade school, we went from 8:00 to 12:00, and twice a week from 2:00 to 4:00, for gym or singing, music, or-- and drawing. And in the gymnasiums, we never-- we went from 8:00 to 1:00-- 1:30 sometimes. Six days a week, never in the afternoon. In the afternoon was religious instructions for Jewish kids who wanted it. So that's-- that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had-- we had-- we had math, and we had German, and we had geography and history and Czech, mostly. You know, Czech grammar, Czech structure, Czech written. What else? I mean, how we started. And drawing, which was my nemesis. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in-- in the second class, we had-- we had geometry, and we had the beginning of algebra. And that's-- and by the grade three of gymnasium, it was Latin names, always. We had Latin and physics. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was tough, so that's why I don't understand always what-- what the fuss is all about here. Nobody asked. You either did it, or you didn't do it. In the gymnasium, you either had-- you-- you didn't get your marks in numbers anymore. You got excellent, very good, good, adequate, non-adequate, at once. And if you had enough excellents to balance with your-- very goods to balance with your excellent-- so you were-- you had distinction. If you had one-- one inadequate, you could write a supplementary exam of-- after the holidays in-- in the gymnasium. And if you passed, you could pass. If you didn't pass, you had to repeat. If you had two inadequates, you were not allowed to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you do, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did very well. I had always distinction. And they published a yearbook every year, and if you had distinction, you had a star behind your name. And, you know, there were always the-- I'm sorry I don't have this. This-- I would-- I have a friend in Switzerland, maybe she has it. She kept-- she was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] serious about it. And with-- with this religion-- with this religious instruction, I remember when we were talking about it, I finally stopped. I was so bored by it, I finally stopped going there. And that was in my second class of gymnasium. And my home professor-- we called him always 'Professor'-- my home professor called me before the end of the school year, and said that my-- the teacher there of religion complained to the rabbi, and the rabbi is failing me in religion. And I'll have to write a supplementary exam, and I certainly will not have my distinction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember like today, I went to him-- to the rabbi, cried bitterly. And he let me go without distinction. He didn't fail me. And then I made my mother sign a piece of paper that I don't have to take religious instruction anymore. That was voluntary, so she-- she did it for me. You entered gymnasium when you were 10 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine. Nine and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine and a half. And you attended gymnasium until--

speaker: interviewee
text: For three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: For three years. You mentioned earlier that there were no other Jewish students in your elementary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my grade school. In this-- in this class, there were two or three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of?

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of 45. What was it like being almost the only Jew, or the only Jew, in a classroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It didn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were friendly with the other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: With everybody, you know? My-- I had two best friends, and-- you know, like girls have best friends. And one of them was the other Jewish girl, and the other one was not. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw each other after school, and that was that. The one who was not, I am still very good friends with her. We walked to-- together everyday to school, and-- because she lived around the corner from me in this Kozi. And we saw each other in Terezin, and she survived. And she's a physician. And in 1968, she went to Switzerland. Her husband is a physician, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was leisure time like for you, growing up in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not very much leisure time, because I had a terribly strict mother and she-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she-- she saw to it that I don't have too much leisure time. I had this governess who was-- who was strict, but she was basically very nice, you know? Even though she was German, she didn't do me any harm. But then in nine-- she was from the Sudetenland, which was, after Munich, annexed. And she had, I remember, such idiocies. Her brother was an architect in-- in one of the border towns. And she had a little nephew, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of whom she was very fond. And she had a sister, and this sister became a nun. And they went to her ordainment, we would call it, or whatever you call it, in Vienna. And she described the ceremony so vividly that I couldn't sleep for a month. And then after, she was with us already in the first address. And she taught me German, and French, and piano. And she went to the park with me we-- when I was little. So in the afternoon, after homework and German and piano, we went to the par-- to the park. Or in winter, we-- first, we went to the park. They were not bad winters, really. It was unpleasant. Sort of nasty, wet. But in the park, I played with all the children. Then we came home. And then it was, again, German, French, and piano. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then was supper and-- and bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, we're going to take a break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Whatever. So what do I do now?
